The next part says most lightning happens inside a cloud, and only a few can be seen jumping between clouds or between earth and cloud. Then it states there are 2000 thunderstorms every minute, producing about 100 lightning strikes per second. The question is asking which statement is true. Let's look at the options: A) Only a small number of lightning flashes occur on earth. Hmm. So A says "only a small number occur on earth," but the passage says 100 per second, so that's a lot. So A is false. B) Lightning flashes usually jump from one cloud to another. The passage says "most lightning flashes take place inside a cloud, and only a few can be seen jumping between two clouds or between earth and a cloud." So "only a few" are between clouds or earth/cloud. So most are inside clouds. So B is not correct because "usually jump from one cloud to another" would mean between clouds, but the passage says only a few are between clouds. So B is false. C) Lightning travels 5 times faster than thunder. The passage mentions that the light reaches eyes almost instantly, while sound takes 5 seconds per mile. So the speed difference is why we see lightning before hearing thunder. But the 5 seconds per mile is the time for sound (thunder) to travel. The question is about the speed ratio. The speed of light is about 186,000 miles per second, while the speed of sound is about 0.2 miles per second (since it takes 5 seconds to travel 1 mile). So the speed of lightning (light) is much faster than thunder (sound), but the exact multiple isn't stated in the passage. The passage doesn't mention 5 times faster, it just mentions the 5 seconds per mile for thunder. So C might be incorrect because the passage doesn't state the ratio is 5 times. The 5 seconds is for the sound to travel 1 mile, not the speed ratio. So C is incorrect. D) There are far more lightning strikes occurring on earth than we can imagine.
